_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 2-2 General Trauma (Revised 05/08/2023, MDHHS Reviewed 2023). Base Michigan template — MCA-specific selections not configured.
protocol_reference: Michigan Trauma and Environmental Protocol Section 2-2: General Trauma — MDHHS Approved 5/8/23, Reviewed 2023
purpose: For severely injured patients meeting trauma triage guidelines, including chest injuries, and patients with symptoms of spinal cord injury, along with extremity weakness, numbness, or sensory loss. Consists of assessment, stabilization, extrication, initiation of resuscitation, and rapid transportation to the closest appropriate trauma facility.

sections.title: General Trauma Management
sections.reference: Michigan Protocol 2-2: General Trauma — General Management
points: Follow General Pre-Hospital Care-Treatment Protocol
Stabilize spinal column while opening the airway; determine level of consciousness — refer to Spinal Injury Assessment-Treatment Protocol
Manage airway and ventilation per Airway Management-Procedure Protocol — AVOID hyperventilation/hyperoxygenation
Control major external bleeding — refer to Bleeding Control (BCON)-Treatment Protocol
If signs of shock are present, refer to Shock-Treatment Protocol
Refer to Mass Casualty Incidents-Special Operations Protocol if appropriate
Determine if the patient is taking blood thinners and document results in the PCR
Initiate transport according to the Adult/Pediatric Trauma Triage-Treatment Protocol or refer to applicable MCA Transport Protocol
Alert receiving hospital as soon as appropriate — include pertinent trauma triage criteria
Obtain vascular access in a manner that will not delay transport (Specialist skill)
Refer to Pain Management-Procedure Protocol

sections.title: Chest Injury
sections.reference: Michigan Protocol 2-2: General Trauma — Chest Injury
points: Control hemorrhage per Bleeding Control (BCON)-Treatment Protocol and Soft Tissue and Orthopedic Injuries-Treatment Protocol
Assess, monitor, and treat life-threatening respiratory problems
Administer high-flow oxygen — avoid positive pressure ventilation if possible

Cover open and/or sucking chest wounds with an occlusive dressing or an FDA-approved, MCA-authorized commercial device; release dressing if worsened shortness of breath or signs of tension pneumothorax
If tension pneumothorax suspected: perform needle decompression per Pleural Decompression-Procedure Protocol (Specialist skill)

sections.title: Abdominal Injury
sections.reference: Michigan Protocol 2-2: General Trauma — Abdominal Injury
points: Cover intestinal eviscerations with a sterile dressing moistened with sterile saline or water; cover the area with an occlusive material (aluminum foil or plastic wrap); cover with a towel or blanket to keep warm; transport with knees slightly bent if possible
DO NOT PUSH VISCERA BACK INTO ABDOMEN
If signs of shock: refer to Shock-Treatment Protocol and/or Hemorrhagic Shock-Treatment Protocol

sections.title: Head Injury
sections.reference: Michigan Protocol 2-2: General Trauma — Head Injury
points: Avoid hypo or hyperventilation
Refer to Head Injury-Treatment Protocol

deterioration_flags: Signs of shock — refer to Shock-Treatment Protocol; obtain vascular access without delaying transport
Tension pneumothorax signs (tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds, distended neck veins, hypotension) — needle decompression (Specialist skill)
Suspected spinal injury — maintain spinal precautions per Spinal Injury Assessment protocol
Altered mental status with head injury — refer to Head Injury protocol; avoid hyperventilation
scope_notes: Scene safety, primary survey, bleeding control, spinal stabilization: all levels
Airway management procedures: refer to Airway Management-Procedure Protocol for scope by level
Pain management: refer to Pain Management-Procedure Protocol for scope by level
